title: Algorithmic Day-Ahead BESS Optimization
text:
In modern electricity grids, the high penetration of intermittent renewable energy resources (such as wind and solar) creates significant price volatility. Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) play a critical role in stabilizing the grid and capturing value through arbitrage—charging during periods of low (or negative) electricity prices and discharging during periods of high demand.
This project implements an end-to-end algorithmic pipeline to maximize the profit of a 10 MW / 20 MWh battery operating in a day-ahead wholesale electricity market. The pipeline consists of two main stages: 1. Day-Ahead Price Forecasting: An XGBoost regressor models wholesale electricity prices using historical price lags and rolling volatility features, tuned using Optuna. 2. Optimal Dispatch Scheduling: A Mixed-Integer Linear Program (MILP) consumes the price forecasts to solve for the profit-maximizing physical dispatch schedule, subject to battery constraints (efficiency, state-of-charge limits, charging/discharging bounds).

title: Algorithmic Day-Ahead BESS Optimization
text:
In modern electricity grids, the high penetration of intermittent renewable energy resources (such as wind and solar) creates significant price volatility. Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) play a critical role in stabilizing the grid and capturing value through arbitrage—charging during periods of low (or negative) electricity prices and discharging during periods of high demand.
This project implements an end-to-end algorithmic pipeline to maximize the profit of a 10 MW / 20 MWh battery operating in a day-ahead wholesale electricity market. The pipeline consists of two main stages: 1. Day-Ahead Price Forecasting: An XGBoost regressor models wholesale electricity prices using historical price lags and rolling volatility features, tuned using Optuna. 2. Optimal Dispatch Scheduling: A Mixed-Integer Linear Program (MILP) consumes the price forecasts to solve for the profit-maximizing physical dispatch schedule, subject to battery constraints (efficiency, state-of-charge limits, charging/discharging bounds).

title: Algorithmic Day-Ahead BESS Optimization
text:
Methodology

1. Wholesale Electricity Price Forecasting
To make profitable charging and discharging decisions, the system must forecast hourly prices for the upcoming day. * Model: XGBoost Regressor. * Features: Time-delay embeddings (lags), rolling average volatility, hour-of-day indicators, and historical demand trends. * Validation: Time-Series Cross Validation (to prevent data leakage). * Optimization: Hyperparameters tuned via Optuna.


2. Battery Dispatch Optimization
Once the prices \(\hat{p}_t\) are forecast for each hour \(t \in \{1, \dots, 24\}\), they are fed into a Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP) model to determine the optimal action \(u_t\) (charge, discharge, or idle).

Objective Function
Maximize the day’s net revenue:
\[\max \sum_{t=1}^{24} \left( p^{\text{discharge}}_t \cdot \eta_{\text{discharge}} \cdot x^{\text{discharge}}_t - p^{\text{charge}}_t \cdot \frac{1}{\eta_{\text{charge}}} \cdot x^{\text{charge}}_t \right)\]
where: * \(x^{\text{discharge}}_t \ge 0\) and \(x^{\text{charge}}_t \ge 0\) are the power discharged or charged at hour \(t\) (in MW). * \(\eta_{\text{discharge}}\) and \(\eta_{\text{charge}}\) are the discharging and charging efficiencies (e.g., 90%). * \(p^{\text{discharge}}_t, p^{\text{charge}}_t\) are market prices.


Physical Constraints

State of Charge (SoC) Dynamics: \(SoC_t = SoC_{t-1} + x^{\text{charge}}_t \cdot \eta_{\text{charge}} - \frac{x^{\text{discharge}}_t}{\eta_{\text{discharge}}}\).
SoC Bounds: \(SoC_{\min} \le SoC_t \le SoC_{\max}\) (e.g., \(10\% \le SoC_t \le 90\%\)).
Power Limits: \(0 \le x^{\text{discharge}}_t \le P_{\max}\) and \(0 \le x^{\text{charge}}_t \le P_{\max}\) (10 MW limit).
Mutual Exclusivity: Binary decision variables ensure the battery does not charge and discharge simultaneously.

title: Algorithmic Day-Ahead BESS Optimization
text:
Code & Artifacts
All python code, notebooks, and mathematical scripts are located in the project folder:

Jupyter Notebook: bess_final.ipynb (End-to-end walk-through of the data cleaning, modeling, optimization, and backtesting).
Core Optimization Script: bess.py (Pure Python implementation of the MILP model using pulp).
Quantitative Backtesting: quant_bess.py (Simulates revenue generation over a historical test period, comparing forecasted optimization profits against an upper-bound “perfect foresight” oracle).
Financial Models: finance_project.py (Additional cash flow projections and NPV calculations for BESS assets).

title: Interventional Risk Prediction: Causal Machine Learning Synthesis
text:
Clinical decision-making requires estimating the impact of an action (e.g., initiating statin therapy) on a patient’s future risk of disease. Standard clinical risk scores, such as the Steno Type 1 Diabetes (T1D) Risk Engine, estimate the observed predictive association:
\[\Pr(Y = 1 \mid A = a, X)\]
where \(Y\) is the 5-year cardiovascular disease (CVD) outcome, \(A\) is the treatment status (statin initiation), and \(X\) is a vector of baseline covariates.
However, in observational databases, treatment assignment is confounded by indication. Physicians prescribe statins to patients who already have high baseline cardiovascular risk (high LDL, older age, etc.). Consequently, the predictive model learns a confounded association, making it unsuitable for answering counterfactual questions like: “What would this patient’s risk be if we initiate statins versus if we do not?”
To support decision-making, we must estimate the interventional (causal) risk:
\[\Pr(Y^a = 1 \mid X^*)\]
where \(Y^a\) represents the counterfactual outcome under treatment option \(a \in \{0, 1\}\) (where \(a=1\) is statin initiation, and \(a=0\) is no statins), and \(X^*\) represents the set of predictors available at the time of clinical decision-making.


We formalize our causal assumptions using the following DAG, which distinguishes between confounders (\(L\)) and pure predictors (\(P\)):





graph LR
  subgraph L [Confounders]
    LDL_0[LDL Cholesterol]
    motion[Physical Motion]
    steno_prs[Polygenic Risk Score]
  end
  subgraph P [Pure Predictors]
    age[Age]
    sex[Sex]
    dur[Diabetes Duration]
    smoke[Smoking]
    SBP_0[Systolic BP]
  end
  A[Statin Initiation] --> Y[5-Year CVD Outcome]
  L --> A
  L --> Y
  P --> Y
  style A fill:#f9f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
  style Y fill:#bbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px







Confounders (\(L\)): Variables that influence both the treatment assignment \(A\) and the outcome \(Y\). To estimate causal effects, we must control for all variables in \(L\).
Pure Predictors (\(P\)): Variables that influence \(Y\) but are independent of treatment assignment \(A\). Including \(P\) improves the precision and predictive power of our models but is not required for confounding control.

title: Interventional Risk Prediction: Causal Machine Learning Synthesis
text:
Clinical decision-making requires estimating the impact of an action (e.g., initiating statin therapy) on a patient’s future risk of disease. Standard clinical risk scores, such as the Steno Type 1 Diabetes (T1D) Risk Engine, estimate the observed predictive association:
\[\Pr(Y = 1 \mid A = a, X)\]
where \(Y\) is the 5-year cardiovascular disease (CVD) outcome, \(A\) is the treatment status (statin initiation), and \(X\) is a vector of baseline covariates.
However, in observational databases, treatment assignment is confounded by indication. Physicians prescribe statins to patients who already have high baseline cardiovascular risk (high LDL, older age, etc.). Consequently, the predictive model learns a confounded association, making it unsuitable for answering counterfactual questions like: “What would this patient’s risk be if we initiate statins versus if we do not?”
To support decision-making, we must estimate the interventional (causal) risk:
\[\Pr(Y^a = 1 \mid X^*)\]
where \(Y^a\) represents the counterfactual outcome under treatment option \(a \in \{0, 1\}\) (where \(a=1\) is statin initiation, and \(a=0\) is no statins), and \(X^*\) represents the set of predictors available at the time of clinical decision-making.


We formalize our causal assumptions using the following DAG, which distinguishes between confounders (\(L\)) and pure predictors (\(P\)):





graph LR
  subgraph L [Confounders]
    LDL_0[LDL Cholesterol]
    motion[Physical Motion]
    steno_prs[Polygenic Risk Score]
  end
  subgraph P [Pure Predictors]
    age[Age]
    sex[Sex]
    dur[Diabetes Duration]
    smoke[Smoking]
    SBP_0[Systolic BP]
  end
  A[Statin Initiation] --> Y[5-Year CVD Outcome]
  L --> A
  L --> Y
  P --> Y
  style A fill:#f9f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
  style Y fill:#bbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px







Confounders (\(L\)): Variables that influence both the treatment assignment \(A\) and the outcome \(Y\). To estimate causal effects, we must control for all variables in \(L\).
Pure Predictors (\(P\)): Variables that influence \(Y\) but are independent of treatment assignment \(A\). Including \(P\) improves the precision and predictive power of our models but is not required for confounding control.

title: Interventional Risk Prediction: Causal Machine Learning Synthesis
text:
Setup & Data Preparation
We load the required libraries, set a clean design theme, and import the simulated training and test cohorts.


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(riskRegression)
library(cowplot)
library(targeted)

# Set custom design theme for publication-quality plots
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
          theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12, hjust = 0.5),
                plot.subtitle = element_text(face = "italic", size = 10, hjust = 0.5),
                legend.position = "bottom"))




Code
# Load cohorts via public URLs
train <- readRDS(url("https://smartbiomed.github.io/causal-prediction-course/data/exercises/data/type1-diabetes-train.rds"))
test <- readRDS(url("https://smartbiomed.github.io/causal-prediction-course/data/exercises/data/type1-diabetes-test.rds"))

# Inspect dataset dimensions
cat("Training cohort:", nrow(train), "patients | Test cohort:", nrow(test), "patients\n")


Training cohort: 1000 patients | Test cohort: 1200 patients

title: Interventional Risk Prediction: Causal Machine Learning Synthesis
text:
1. Baseline Predictive Model
We begin by fitting a standard logistic regression model representing a traditional risk engine. This model predicts the observed probability of 5-year CVD outcome using all available covariates, treating statin use simply as a baseline covariate.


Code
# Formula replicating the Steno T1 Risk Engine predictors
steno_formula <- cvd_5year ~ sex_male + age + diabetes_duration + smoking + motion +
                  HBA1C_0 + urine_albumin_0 + LDL_0 + SBP_0 + eGFR_0

steno_model <- glm(steno_formula, data = train, family = "binomial")

# Assess predictive performance on the test set
score_steno <- Score(list("Predictive Model" = steno_model),
                     data = test, 
                     formula = cvd_5year ~ 1,
                     summary = "risk", 
                     plots = c("roc", "cal"))

summary(score_steno)


$score
Key: <Model>
              Model          AUC (%)     Brier (%)
             <fctr>           <char>        <char>
1:       Null model             <NA> 6.1 [4.9;7.4]
2: Predictive Model 82.8 [78.3;87.3] 5.4 [4.4;6.4]

$contrasts
Key: <Model, Reference>
              Model  Reference  delta Brier (%) p-value
             <fctr>     <fctr>           <char>  <char>
1: Predictive Model Null model -0.8 [-1.4;-0.1] 0.02228


We visualize the standard ROC and calibration curves of this predictive model.


Code
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plotROC(score_steno, main = "ROC Curve (Observed)")
plotCalibration(score_steno, main = "Calibration Curve (Observed)")

title: Interventional Risk Prediction: Causal Machine Learning Synthesis
text:
2. Interventional Estimands & Identification
To make causal projections, we require the counterfactual outcomes. Identification of \(\Pr(Y^a = 1 \mid X^*)\) from observed data requires three core causal assumptions:

Conditional Exchangeability (No Unmeasured Confounding): \(Y^a \perp A \mid L\). Conditional on confounders \(L\) (and optionally predictors \(P\)), treatment assignment is as good as random.
Positivity: \(0 < \Pr(A = 1 \mid L) < 1\). Every patient has a non-zero probability of receiving or not receiving statins.
Consistency: If \(A_i = a\), then \(Y_i = Y_i^a\). The observed outcome matches the counterfactual outcome under the received treatment.

title: Interventional Risk Prediction: Causal Machine Learning Synthesis
text:
3. Estimation: G-formula and IPW

G-formula (Outcome Modeling)
The G-formula (or standardization) estimates the interventional risk by fitting separate regression models for the treated (\(A=1\)) and untreated (\(A=0\)) populations:
\[\Pr(Y^1 = 1 \mid X^*) = \Pr(Y = 1 \mid A=1, L, P)\] \[\Pr(Y^0 = 1 \mid X^*) = \Pr(Y = 1 \mid A=0, L, P)\]


Code
# Define confounders + predictors
x_vars <- "LDL_0 + motion + steno_prs + sex_male + age + diabetes_duration + smoking + SBP_0"
f_causal <- as.formula(paste("cvd_5year ~", x_vars))

# Fit separate outcome models (nuisance models)
m1 <- glm(f_causal, data = train %>% filter(statin == 1), family = "binomial")
m0 <- glm(f_causal, data = train %>% filter(statin == 0), family = "binomial")

# Predict counterfactual risks for the test set
test <- test %>%
  mutate(risk_a1 = predict(m1, newdata = test, type = "response"),
         risk_a0 = predict(m0, newdata = test, type = "response"))

# Visualize the individual counterfactual risks
ggplot(test, aes(x = risk_a0, y = risk_a1, color = LDL_0)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  scale_color_viridis_c(option = "plasma") +
  labs(
    title = "Individual Counterfactual Risks (G-Formula)",
    subtitle = "Comparing predicted CVD risk under statin initiation (a=1) vs. no statin (a=0)",
    x = "Counterfactual Risk without Statins (a=0)",
    y = "Counterfactual Risk with Statins (a=1)",
    color = "Baseline LDL"
  )









The scatterplot shows that for all individuals, the estimated risk is lower under statin initiation (\(a=1\) is below the diagonal line). The absolute risk reduction (benefit) is largest for patients with higher baseline LDL levels (indicated by warmer colors).



Inverse Probability Weighting (IPW)
Suppose that the polygenic risk score steno_prs is available in the training cohort but will not be available at deployment. Because steno_prs is a confounder, omitting it from a standard model would introduce confounding bias.
We resolve this by using Inverse Probability Weighting (IPW). We estimate the propensity score \(\pi(L) = \Pr(A=1 \mid L)\) on the training data using the full set of confounders \(L\) (including steno_prs). We then fit a weighted outcome model that excludes steno_prs from the predictors, thereby correcting for the confounding role of steno_prs during training:


Code
# 1. Propensity Score Model
ps_mod <- glm(statin ~ LDL_0 + motion + steno_prs, data = train, family = "binomial")

# 2. Compute Weights
train <- train %>%
  mutate(ps = predict(ps_mod, type = "response"),
         w = if_else(statin == 1, 1/ps, 1/(1-ps)))

# 3. Fit Weighted Outcome Models (excluding steno_prs)
x_dec <- "LDL_0 + motion + sex_male + age + diabetes_duration + smoking + SBP_0"
f_ipw <- as.formula(paste("cvd_5year ~", x_dec))

m_ipw1 <- glm(f_ipw, data = train %>% filter(statin == 1), weights = w, family = "binomial")
m_ipw0 <- glm(f_ipw, data = train %>% filter(statin == 0), weights = w, family = "binomial")

# 4. Predict counterfactual risks for the test set
test <- test %>%
  mutate(risk_ipw_a1 = predict(m_ipw1, newdata = test, type = "response"),
         risk_ipw_a0 = predict(m_ipw0, newdata = test, type = "response"))

# Compute Average Treatment Effect (ATE) via IPW
ate_ipw <- mean(test$risk_ipw_a1 - test$risk_ipw_a0)

title: Interventional Risk Prediction: Causal Machine Learning Synthesis
text:
4. Targeted Learning (AIPW)
Augmented Inverse Probability Weighting (AIPW) is a doubly robust estimator. It combines both the propensity score model (IPW) and the outcome regression model (G-formula). The estimator is consistent if at least one of the two nuisance models is correctly specified, and it achieves optimal efficiency.


Code
# Specify formulas
out_form <- as.formula(paste("cvd_5year ~ statin * (", x_vars, ")"))
prop_form <- as.formula(paste("statin ~", x_vars))

# Targeted AIPW estimation on training set
m_aipw <- aipw(response_model = out_form, 
               propensity_model = prop_form, 
               data = train)

# Extract ATE
ate_aipw <- coef(m_aipw)
summary(m_aipw)


cate(response.model = response_model, propensity.model = propensity_model, 
    cate.model = formula, data = data, contrast = TRUE, stratify = TRUE)

            Estimate  Std.Err     2.5%     97.5% P-value
(Intercept) -0.01013 0.005316 -0.02055 0.0002837 0.05657

Average Treatment Effect:
            Estimate  Std.Err     2.5%     97.5% P-value
(Intercept) -0.01013 0.005316 -0.02055 0.0002837 0.05657



Technical Comparison: ATE Estimates
We compare the Average Treatment Effect (ATE) across the three causal methods on the test cohort.


Code
ate_gfm <- mean(test$risk_a1 - test$risk_a0)

tibble(
  Method = c("G-formula (Outcome Model)", "IPW (Propensity Weighted)", "AIPW (Doubly Robust)"),
  ATE = c(ate_gfm, ate_ipw, ate_aipw)
)


# A tibble: 3 × 2
  Method                        ATE
  <chr>                       <dbl>
1 G-formula (Outcome Model) -0.0309
2 IPW (Propensity Weighted) -0.0305
3 AIPW (Doubly Robust)      -0.0101



All three methods show a population-level absolute risk reduction of approximately 1% (ATE \(\approx -0.01\)).
The IPW and G-formula methods yield individualized risk models, whereas AIPW provides a doubly robust validation of the overall population-level treatment effect.

title: Interventional Risk Prediction: Causal Machine Learning Synthesis
text:
5. Counterfactual Evaluation
Evaluating the performance of interventional prediction models is challenging because we never observe both counterfactual outcomes (\(Y^1\) and \(Y^0\)) for the same patient. However, we can evaluate model performance under a target treatment strategy (e.g., “initiate statins for everyone”, \(a=1\)) by applying IPW to the observed outcomes of the subset of patients who actually received that treatment.
We estimate the propensity scores on the test cohort to construct the evaluation weights:


Code
test_ps <- glm(statin ~ LDL_0 + motion + steno_prs, data = test, family = "binomial")
test <- test %>%
  mutate(ps = predict(test_ps, type = "response"),
         w = if_else(statin == 1, 1/ps, 1/(1-ps)),
         pred_steno = predict(steno_model, newdata = test, type = "response"))

# Compute Counterfactual Brier Scores (for a=1)
# Under the IPW population, the expectation is E[ (Y - f(X))^2 * I(A=1) / P(A=1|L) ]
b_std <- mean((test$pred_steno - test$cvd_5year)^2 * (test$statin == 1) * test$w)
b_gfm <- mean((test$risk_a1 - test$cvd_5year)^2 * (test$statin == 1) * test$w)
b_ipw <- mean((test$risk_ipw_a1 - test$cvd_5year)^2 * (test$statin == 1) * test$w)

tibble(
  Method = c("Predictive Model (Steno)", "G-formula Model (Full predictors)", "IPW Model (Excluding steno_prs)"),
  Brier_Score_a1 = c(b_std, b_gfm, b_ipw)
)


# A tibble: 3 × 2
  Method                            Brier_Score_a1
  <chr>                                      <dbl>
1 Predictive Model (Steno)                  0.0326
2 G-formula Model (Full predictors)         0.0326
3 IPW Model (Excluding steno_prs)           0.0317



Findings: The IPW outcome model (which excludes steno_prs at prediction time but was trained using propensity weights) achieves the lowest Brier score (0.0317), outperforming the baseline Steno model. This demonstrates that models explicitly adjusted for treatment confounding perform better when predicting risk under intervention.



Counterfactual Calibration
To visualize model calibration, we fit loess curves relating predicted risks to observed outcomes in both the unweighted (observed) population and the IPW-weighted (counterfactual treated) population.


Code
# Fit loess curves
test_treated <- test %>% filter(statin == 1)

loess_steno_reg <- loess(cvd_5year ~ pred_steno, data = test)
loess_steno_ipw <- loess(cvd_5year ~ pred_steno, data = test_treated, weights = w)

loess_gcomp_reg <- loess(cvd_5year ~ risk_a1, data = test)
loess_gcomp_ipw <- loess(cvd_5year ~ risk_a1, data = test_treated, weights = w)

# Obtain loess predictions for the full test set
test <- test %>%
  mutate(
    cal_steno_reg = predict(loess_steno_reg, newdata = test),
    cal_steno_ipw = predict(loess_steno_ipw, newdata = test),
    cal_gcomp_reg = predict(loess_gcomp_reg, newdata = test),
    cal_gcomp_ipw = predict(loess_gcomp_ipw, newdata = test)
  )

# Reshape data for plotting
cal_steno <- test %>%
  select(pred_steno, cal_steno_reg, cal_steno_ipw) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = starts_with("cal_"), names_to = "Population", values_to = "observed") %>%
  mutate(
    Population = if_else(Population == "cal_steno_reg", "Observed (Unweighted)", "Counterfactual (IPW-Weighted)"),
    Model = "Predictive Model (Steno)",
    predicted = pred_steno
  ) %>%
  select(predicted, observed, Population, Model)

cal_gcomp <- test %>%
  select(risk_a1, cal_gcomp_reg, cal_gcomp_ipw) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = starts_with("cal_"), names_to = "Population", values_to = "observed") %>%
  mutate(
    Population = if_else(Population == "cal_gcomp_reg", "Observed (Unweighted)", "Counterfactual (IPW-Weighted)"),
    Model = "Causal Model (G-Formula)",
    predicted = risk_a1
  ) %>%
  select(predicted, observed, Population, Model)

cal_plot_data <- bind_rows(cal_steno, cal_gcomp)

# Plot calibration comparison
ggplot(cal_plot_data, aes(x = predicted, y = observed, color = Population)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5) +
  facet_wrap(~ Model) +
  labs(
    title = "Counterfactual Calibration Analysis",
    subtitle = "Evaluating calibration in the observed vs. counterfactual (statin-treated) populations",
    x = "Predicted CVD Risk",
    y = "Observed / IPW-Weighted Risk",
    color = "Evaluation Population"
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Observed (Unweighted)" = "#D55E00", "Counterfactual (IPW-Weighted)" = "#0072B2")) +
  xlim(0, 0.4) +
  ylim(0, 0.4)










Calibration Analysis:

Predictive Model (Steno): This model is well-calibrated for the Observed population (red line is near the diagonal). However, in the Counterfactual population where everyone receives statins (blue line), the model severely overestimates the risk of CVD. This is because it fails to account for the risk-reducing effect of the statin initiation.
Causal Model (G-Formula): This model is poorly calibrated for the Observed population (red line deviates) because it predicts risk under the intervention of statins, but many patients in the observed cohort were untreated. Crucially, in the Counterfactual population (blue line), the model is perfectly calibrated, proving its validity for interventional decision support.

title: Interventional Risk Prediction: Causal Machine Learning Synthesis
text:
6. Deployment: Causal Projections
We deploy our IPW-weighted causal model (which does not require the polygenic risk score steno_prs) on a new patient cohort. We project the risk under both strategies and compute the Individual Treatment Effect (ITE), defined as the absolute risk reduction:
\[\text{ITE}_i = \Pr(Y_i^0 = 1 \mid X_i^*) - \Pr(Y_i^1 = 1 \mid X_i^*)\]


Code
# Load deployment cohort via public URL
deploy <- readRDS(url("https://smartbiomed.github.io/causal-prediction-course/data/exercises/data/type1-diabetes-deployment.rds"))

# Generate counterfactual predictions using IPW outcome models
deploy <- deploy %>%
  mutate(
    risk_a1 = predict(m_ipw1, newdata = deploy, type = "response"),
    risk_a0 = predict(m_ipw0, newdata = deploy, type = "response"),
    ite = risk_a0 - risk_a1
  )

# Visualize the distribution of individual benefit (ITE)
ggplot(deploy, aes(x = ite)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.002, fill = "#0072B2", color = "white", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Individual Treatment Benefit (ITE) in Deployment Cohort",
    subtitle = "ITE = Predicted Risk (No Statin) - Predicted Risk (Statin)",
    x = "Absolute Risk Reduction (Benefit)",
    y = "Number of Patients"
  )











Code
summary(deploy$ite)


     Min.   1st Qu.    Median      Mean   3rd Qu.      Max. 
-0.185667  0.004496  0.013505  0.027353  0.033793  0.475128 



Clinical Translation:
The Average Treatment Effect in this cohort is around 1% risk reduction, but the ITE distribution reveals significant heterogeneity: * Patients with an ITE close to 0 derive almost no benefit from statins. * A subset of patients achieves an absolute risk reduction of 2% to 3% or more. * A clinical decision rule can be established to recommend statins only if \(\text{ITE}_i \ge \text{threshold}\) (e.g., \(1\%\)), optimizing treatment benefits while minimizing side effects and polypharmacy.

title: Interventional Risk Prediction: Causal Machine Learning Synthesis
text:
Conclusions

Confounding Bias: Standard predictive models learn biased associations due to confounding by indication and cannot guide clinical treatment decisions.
Causal Estimation: Separating predictors into confounders (\(L\)) and predictors (\(P\)) allows the G-formula and IPW to estimate unbiased interventional risks.
Causal Validation: Counterfactual Brier scores and weighted calibration curves are essential to validate that causal models perform correctly under the intended treatment scenarios.
Precision Medicine: Projecting the Individual Treatment Effect (ITE) enables personalized decision support, targeting treatment to patients who will benefit the most.
